_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Respiratory: Tracheostomy Management. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Respiratory: Tracheostomy Management

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Respiratory: Tracheostomy Management
points: Meet airway management goals in a patient with a tracheostomy a. Assure patent airway, understand how to troubleshoot tracheostomy in a patient with respiratory distress
b. Assure adequate oxygenation and ventilation

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Any adult or pediatric patient with an existing tracheostomy greater than 7 days post placement and a mature stoma tract
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Adult or pediatric patient with tracheostomy less than 7 days post placement (i.e., no mature stoma tract)

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Especially in pediatric tracheostomy patients with significant respiratory distress, plugging or dislodgement of the tracheostomy is the problem until proven otherwise. Signs and symptoms of respiratory distress, cyanosis, ventilator alarms sounding, decreased level of consciousness, decreased SpO2 or cardiac arrest in patients with a tracheostomy, as well as bradycardia in pediatric tracheostomy patients should be presumed due to a tracheostomy obstruction

Laryngectomy patients and some patients with congenital or surgical airway abnormalities cannot be orally intubated. Patients with tracheostomy alone (e.g., for mechanical ventilation) and no airway abnormalities should be able to be orally intubated

For recent tracheostomy patients who present with bleeding from the tracheostomy in the early (up to 3 weeks) postoperative period, a tracheoinnominate arterial bleed is an uncommon and life-threatening complication (0.7% incidence and a 90% mortality rate) a. 50% of these patients present initially with a smaller sentinel bleed/hemoptysis which appears to have stopped
Prompt tracheostomy replacement is important. Delays allow for narrowing of the stoma and can make recannulation more difficult

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Tracheostomy tube components a. Outer cannula: the tracheostomy size is stamped on the collar
To determine the appropriate size suction catheter, double the size of the tracheostomy (number on collar of tracheostomy tube)
A bougie may aid in the placement of an endotracheal tube into a mature stoma
An inner cannula may be required to ventilate through the tracheostomy tube
Uncuffed and fenestrated cuffed tracheostomy tubes may not protect the patient from aspiration

If transporting a patient with a tracheostomy either in an emergency or routine transport, the patients home tracheostomy equipment (e.g., 'Go bag') should accompany them. The equipment that needs to be at the bedside to ensure safety includes appropriately sized French suction catheters, operating suction system, and spare tracheostomy tubes. Sterile saline, sterile gloves and water-soluble medical lubrication packets should also be available. Most tracheostomy patients will maintain a kit with these supplies to travel with

Inadvertent tracheostomy decannulation incidence is the second most frequent life-threatening pediatric tracheostomy complication, occurring at rates of 0.35'15%, with the vast majority occurring more than 7 days postoperatively
Tracheostomy obstruction can occur for several reasons, including mucus plugging, abnormal/excess granulation tissue, tracheomalacia causing collapse of the tracheal wall around the tube

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Especially in pediatric tracheostomy patients with significant respiratory distress, plugging or dislodgement of the tracheostomy is the problem until proven otherwise. Signs and symptoms of respiratory distress, cyanosis, ventilator alarms sounding, decreased level of consciousness, decreased SpO2 or cardiac arrest in patients with a tracheostomy, as well as bradycardia in pediatric tracheostomy patients should be presumed due to a tracheostomy obstruction

For recent tracheostomy patients who present with bleeding from the tracheostomy in the early (up to 3 weeks) postoperative period, a tracheoinnominate arterial bleed is an uncommon and life-threatening complication (0.7% incidence and a 90% mortality rate) a. 50% of these patients present initially with a smaller sentinel bleed/hemoptysis which appears to have stopped
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
